title: Editing Tradeoffs: Fidelity vs. Change
concept: Stronger edits change more of the image but risk losing the original's identity; weaker edits preserve identity but may under-apply the requested change. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: Text-to-Video Generation.'

exercises.prompt: You ask an AI to edit a photo of your dog to 'add sunglasses.' What's the risk if the edit is TOO strong?

options.text: The dog might barely look like your dog anymore
The sunglasses will be too small
The photo will become black and white

exercises.explain: Strong edits change a lot of the image — sometimes so much that the original dog's identity gets lost in the process!

cards.title: Walking a Tightrope
cards.body: Every AI image edit is a balancing act. Push too hard, and the picture transforms into something new — but not quite 'yours' anymore. Push too little, and your request barely shows up at all. Today we explore that tricky middle ground: fidelity vs. change.

cards.title: Fidelity vs. Change
cards.body: Fidelity means how much the edited image still looks like the original. Change means how much the AI actually modified it. Stronger edits boost change but can hurt fidelity — the picture starts losing its original identity. Weaker edits protect fidelity but may barely apply your requested change.

cards.title: Renovating a House
cards.body: Imagine renovating your house. A light touch-up (new paint) keeps it recognizably YOUR house — but maybe doesn't fix everything you wanted. A total gut renovation gets everything changed just right, but afterward, it barely feels like the same home anymore. AI edits face this exact tradeoff!

cards.title: Adding a Hat
cards.body: Ask an AI to 'add a red hat' to a portrait. A weak edit setting might place a faint, barely-visible hat, keeping the face perfectly intact. A strong edit setting might add a bold hat — but could also reshape the face or background so much the person looks different.

cards.title: Myth: Bigger Change Is Always Better
cards.body: Myth: Stronger edits are always the better choice since they follow instructions more closely. Truth: Stronger edits can accidentally erase details you wanted to keep — like someone's face, a logo, or a background. The 'best' edit strength depends on what you're trying to protect versus what you're trying to change.

cards.title: Two Edit Strengths, Two Outcomes
cards.body: Compare what happens when you apply the SAME instruction with different edit strengths.

left.text: High fidelity — looks like the original, but the change is barely visible or incomplete

right.text: Big visible change — but risks losing the original identity and details

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a risk of using a VERY strong edit setting?

options.text: The image might lose its original identity
The edit will take longer to load
The image will become smaller in file size
The colors will always turn grayscale

exercises.explain: Strong edits change a lot of the image, which can make it stop resembling the original photo — that's the fidelity risk.

exercises.prompt: A weak edit setting always fully applies your requested change while keeping the original perfectly intact.
exercises.explain: False! Weak edits protect the original's identity, but they often under-apply the requested change — meaning your edit might barely show up.

exercises.sentence: Stronger edits change more of the image but risk losing the original's ___.
exercises.explain: The key tradeoff is identity — strong edits can change the image so much it no longer looks like the original subject.

exercises.prompt: You want to edit a photo of your best friend to 'make them smile more,' but it's VERY important their face still looks like them. Which edit strength should you choose?

options.text: A weaker edit, to preserve fidelity to the original face
The strongest possible edit, to guarantee the smile shows
It doesn't matter which strength you pick

exercises.explain: Since identity matters most here, you'd choose a weaker edit to protect fidelity — even if the smile change ends up more subtle.

exercises.prompt: An artist edits a photo of a historic building to 'add graffiti art.' They want the graffiti to be clearly visible, AND the building must still be recognizable as the same historic landmark. What's the smartest approach?

options.text: Use the strongest edit setting possible to make sure the graffiti is bold
Use the weakest edit setting possible so nothing changes
Find a balanced edit strength that adds visible graffiti while keeping the building's key features intact
Skip the edit entirely since tradeoffs can't be managed

exercises.explain: Great work! The smartest approach is finding balance — enough change to see the graffiti clearly, but not so much that the building loses its recognizable identity. That's the fidelity vs. change tradeoff in action. Next up: Text-to-Video Generation.
